planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in full sun for darkest foliage.

directions: Nearly black-purple leaves give this canna a moody, dramatic look topped by scarlet-orange flowers. Plant rhizomes in full sun to keep the foliage as dark as possible. Water steadily and feed monthly through the growing season.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost where dark leaves stand out.

directions: Glossy near-black burgundy leaves are the hallmark of this striking canna, set off by deep red flowers. Plant rhizomes 4 inches deep and grow in full sun for the richest leaf color. Its shiny foliage looks clean and polished all summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in rich, moist soil.

directions: An old heirloom variety with green leaves and warm coral-pink flowers. Plant rhizomes shallowly in fertile soil and keep well watered. This dependable bloomer flowers freely from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in a sunny bed.

directions: This showy bicolor has red flowers boldly bordered in golden yellow above green leaves. Plant rhizomes 4 inches deep in moist, rich soil. Snap off faded flower heads to keep the plant blooming into fall.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in full sun.

directions: Large soft-yellow flowers freckled with orange-red spots crown this classic canna. It occasionally reverts to solid red flowers on some stems. Plant rhizomes in fertile soil and water well for a steady summer display.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost for bronze foliage and red bloom.

directions: A tall, robust heirloom with bronze-red foliage and big orange-scarlet flowers. Plant rhizomes 4 to 6 inches deep in rich soil and give room to reach full height. Its stature makes it a fine screen or backdrop plant.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in a sunny, moist spot.

directions: Bright yellow flowers heavily speckled with red spots give this canna a playful look above fresh green leaves. Plant rhizomes shallowly in rich soil and keep watered through summer heat. Remove old blooms to prolong the flowering season.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in beds or containers.

directions: This compact canna carries red flowers rimmed with a bright yellow edge, perfect for pots and small beds. Plant rhizomes 3 to 4 inches deep in fertile soil. Its short stature keeps it tidy without staking.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost with afternoon shade to protect leaves.

directions: Grown for green leaves splashed with irregular white variegation, this tall canna makes a bold statement. The white areas can scorch, so give it afternoon shade and steady moisture. Soft orange flowers appear late in the season above the striking foliage.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in full sun for brightest leaf color.

directions: Leaves striped in burgundy, pink, and gold make this canna nearly as colorful as its bright orange flowers. Plant rhizomes shallowly in rich soil and site in full sun for the most vivid stripes. Feed and water generously through the summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in a warm, sunny bed.

directions: This tall, graceful canna bears narrow, spidery flowers in soft apricot and pale pink shades. The slender blooms give it an airy, delicate look unusual for a canna. Plant rhizomes in rich soil and stake the tall stems in exposed sites.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in full sun.

directions: Warm apricot-yellow flowers glow against bronze-tinged foliage on this mid-sized canna. Plant rhizomes 4 inches deep in fertile, moist soil. Deadhead spent spikes to keep the flowers coming through late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in a sunny location.

directions: Dusky purple-bronze leaves make a fine foil for the soft pink flowers of this refined canna. Plant rhizomes in rich soil and grow in full sun for the best foliage color. Its subtle color pairing suits softer garden schemes.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost where the narrow upright leaves can shine.

directions: Prized for slender, upright purple-bronze leaves, this tall canna has an elegant, architectural form. Small salmon-orange flowers appear high above the foliage in late summer. Plant rhizomes in rich soil and use it as a vertical accent in beds or large pots.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in deep, rich, moist soil.

directions: This tall canna produces graceful pendant clusters of deep rose-pink flowers on arching stems. Give it deep, fertile soil and constant moisture to fuel its height. The nodding blooms and large blue-green leaves lend a lush, tropical feel.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in full sun.

directions: A Victorian heirloom bearing golden-yellow flowers heavily dotted with red-orange spots. Plant rhizomes 4 inches deep in rich, moist soil. This vigorous variety blooms freely and reaches an impressive height by late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in a moist, sunny bed.

directions: Clear canary-yellow flowers, sometimes lightly speckled, sit above fresh green leaves. Plant rhizomes shallowly in fertile soil and water freely in summer. Remove faded spikes to keep the bright yellow blooms coming.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in beds or containers.

directions: This compact canna carries yellow flowers freckled with red-orange spots over green foliage. Its shorter height makes it ideal for pots and the front of a border. Plant rhizomes 3 to 4 inches deep and keep evenly watered.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in full sun for rich foliage.

directions: Bronze-purple leaves with a rosy cast set off the soft coral-pink flowers of this handsome canna. Plant rhizomes in rich soil and grow in full sun to deepen the foliage tones. It is a strong, dependable grower through the heat of summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in full sun.

directions: Deep bronze-purple foliage supports velvety dark red flowers on this tall, dramatic canna. Plant rhizomes 4 to 6 inches deep in rich soil and grow in full sun. Its height and dark coloring make a commanding accent in large borders.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in a sunny, sheltered spot.

directions: This dwarf canna is grown for leaves striped in pink, green, and gold that glow in the sun. Coral-pink flowers add to the warm color show. Plant rhizomes shallowly and keep in a bright, protected spot for the best foliage.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost where striped leaves catch the light.

directions: Green leaves finely striped in creamy yellow make this canna eye-catching even before it blooms. Red buds open to yellow flowers marked with orange. Plant rhizomes in rich soil and give full sun to keep the leaf stripes crisp.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in pots or the front of a bed.

directions: A tidy dwarf canna bearing soft coral-pink flowers over blue-green leaves. Its compact habit makes it well suited to containers and small gardens. Plant rhizomes 3 to 4 inches deep and keep steadily moist through summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in a sunny bed or large pot.

directions: This compact canna produces a steady flush of bright orange flowers with a golden throat above green leaves. Its shorter height and heavy bloom make it a strong container plant. Plant rhizomes shallowly and deadhead to keep flowers coming until frost.

planting.note: Start seed indoors well before the last frost and set out once warm.

directions: This award-winning seed canna blooms the first year from an early indoor start. Nick and soak the hard seeds, then sow warm about eight weeks before the last frost. Rose-pink flowers rise over green leaves from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Start seed indoors early and transplant after frost.

directions: This award-winning seed canna produces uniform plants topped with vivid scarlet flowers. Scarify and soak the seeds, then sow warm about eight weeks before the last frost. It flowers reliably the first year and is self-cleaning, needing little deadheading.

planting.note: Start seed indoors early and set out after frost.

directions: Part of the vigorous Cannova seed series, this variety pairs bronze-tinged foliage with bright scarlet flowers. Nick and soak seeds, then start warm indoors about eight weeks before the last frost. It blooms early and heavily the first season.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in full sun for crisp variegation.

directions: Green leaves boldly striped in yellow give this canna a bright, cheerful look before it even blooms. Red buds open to soft yellow flowers. Plant rhizomes in rich soil and grow in full sun to keep the leaf stripes clean and distinct.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in rich, moist soil and full sun.

directions: This garden hybrid group covers the familiar large-flowered cannas in shades of red, orange, yellow, and pink. Plant rhizomes 4 inches deep in fertile, moist soil and full sun. Deadhead spent spikes and lift rhizomes in fall where winters are too cold for them to survive.

planting.note: Wait for warm, settled soil before setting out tubers.

directions: Plant tubers two inches deep in loose, humus-rich soil after the ground has warmed. Provide dappled shade and steady moisture to keep the bright white leaves crisp. Its snowy foliage with dark green veining fills out within about two months.

planting.note: Plant in warm soil after spring frosts end.

directions: Set tubers two inches deep in loose, organic soil once warmth arrives. This compact, sun-tolerant variety holds up well in containers with regular watering. Deep red leaves bordered by a wide green edge emerge over six to eight weeks.

planting.note: Plant after the soil warms well past frost.

directions: Bury tubers two inches deep in rich, moisture-holding soil once it is warm. Keep the bed damp and shaded from scorching afternoon sun. Deep red leaves with a narrow green margin fill out over six to eight weeks.

planting.note: Plant tubers in warm soil after spring frosts.

directions: Plant tubers two inches deep in moisture-retentive, organic soil once warm. Keep in dappled shade with steady water for the best leaf color. Leaves show a rosy-pink center fading to white with a green border over about two months.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost passes and soil warms.

directions: Bury tubers two inches deep in fertile, moisture-holding soil once warm. Keep evenly watered in bright shade to preserve the delicate coloring. Green leaves washed with pink and dark pink veins fill out over about two months.

planting.note: Plant compact tubers once the soil is warm.

directions: Plant the small tubers an inch and a half deep in loose, damp soil after frost. This compact form of Candidum suits pots and shady edges. White leaves with green veins mature within about six weeks.

planting.note: Plant corms after the last frost when soil is warm.

directions: Plant corms four to six inches deep in rich, constantly moist soil, even at a pond edge. Feed heavily and never let the roots dry out for the biggest leaves. Broad heart-shaped green leaves reach full size within three to five months.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost when the soil warms.

directions: Plant corms four to six inches deep in rich, wet soil after the frost date. Grow in full to part sun with abundant water for the boldest speckling. Each green leaf is randomly splashed with black and purple as the plant matures over several months.

planting.note: Plant after frost once the ground is warm.

directions: Plant corms four to six inches deep in constantly moist, fertile soil after warming. The cupped leaves collect rain and tip over on dark stems, so give it plenty of water. Small folded leaves on tall purple petioles develop over three to five months.

planting.note: Set out corms after frost and once soil warms.

directions: Plant corms four to six inches deep in rich, wet soil after the last frost. Give it steady moisture and sun to intensify the metallic sheen. Glossy blue-black leaves with a rippled texture mature over three to five months.

planting.note: Plant corms in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant corms four to six inches deep in moist, fertile soil once warm. This is one of the hardiest elephant ears and often survives cooler winters with a mulch. Green leaves on pink-blushed stems fill out over three to five months.

planting.note: Plant after frost when soil is warm.

directions: Set corms four to six inches deep in rich, wet soil after the frost date. Keep the roots wet and feed generously for tall, dramatic growth. Dark green leaves on purple-black stems mature over three to five months.

planting.note: Set out corms after frost once soil is warm.

directions: Plant corms four to six inches deep in fertile, constantly moist soil after warming. Give bright light and steady water to bring out the golden tones. Chartreuse-gold leaves fill out over three to five months.

planting.note: Plant after frost in warm, rich soil for maximum size.

directions: Plant the corm four to six inches deep in deep, fertile, always-moist soil after warming. Feed heavily and water constantly to push its enormous leaves. Pale green leaves several feet across develop through a long four-to-six-month season.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost when soil is warm.

directions: Plant the rhizome just below the surface in rich, moist but well-drained soil after warming. Unlike Colocasia it prefers drainage, so avoid standing water. Huge glossy leaves held upright on thick stems develop over four to six months.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Set the rhizome just below the soil surface in rich, moist ground after warming. Provide part shade and steady water for lush upright growth. Large ribbed leaves develop over four to six months, and mature plants may bloom with a fragrant spathe.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost once soil warms.

directions: Plant the rhizome just under the surface in rich, moist, well-drained soil after warming. This vigorous hybrid is fast and sturdy, taking sun with plenty of water. Ruffled upright leaves on ridged stems develop over four to six months.

planting.note: Grow warm; mostly a houseplant outside the tropics.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly in a chunky, well-drained mix and keep it warm and humid. Water when the top inch dries and avoid soggy soil, which rots the roots. Glossy dark leaves with bold white veins and scalloped edges develop over several months.

planting.note: Keep warm and humid; grow as a houseplant in cool climates.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly in a loose, fast-draining mix and give it warmth and high humidity. Water when the surface dries and provide bright indirect light. Velvety dark green arrow leaves with sharp white veins develop over several months.

planting.note: Grow warm and humid; a compact houseplant in cool areas.

directions: Plant the small rhizome shallowly in an airy, well-drained mix and keep it warm. This dwarf jewel dislikes wet feet, so water only when the top dries. Thick near-black velvety leaves with silver veins develop slowly over several months.

planting.note: Grow warm and humid; a compact houseplant in most climates.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly in a very open, well-draining mix and keep humidity high. Water when the surface dries and shelter from direct sun. Stiff silvery-green leaves with deeply textured, scale-like veining develop over several months.

planting.note: Keep warm and humid; grow as a houseplant in cool climates.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly in a loose, quick-draining mix and provide warmth and humidity. Water when the top dries and give bright indirect light for the best sheen. Metallic coppery leaves with dark raised veins develop over several months.

planting.note: Grow warm and humid; a houseplant outside the tropics.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly in an airy, well-drained mix with steady warmth and humidity. Water when the top inch dries and keep out of direct sun. Deeply lobed dark leaves edged and veined in silvery-white develop over several months.

common_name: Hardy Elephant Ear / New Guinea Shield

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in warm soil.

directions: Plant the rhizome just below the surface in rich, well-drained soil after warming. This tougher species tolerates part shade and cooler nights than most alocasias. Thick green leaves with bronze-purple undersides develop over four to six months.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost when soil is warm.

directions: Plant the rhizome just below the surface in rich, moist but well-drained soil after warming. Give it warmth, bright light, and regular water for strong growth. Uniquely shaped leaves with a long tail resembling a stingray develop over four to six months.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant the rhizome just under the surface in rich, moist, well-drained soil after warming. This large hybrid is vigorous and upright, taking part sun with ample water. Big glossy green paddle leaves develop over four to six months.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost once soil warms.

directions: Plant the rhizome just below the surface in rich, moist but well-drained soil after warming. This compact species suits smaller beds and large containers. Bright green ruffled leaves on short stems develop over four to six months.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions out after frost danger passes in spring.

directions: Set the crown just below the soil surface in rich, well-drained ground with the roots spread out. Prized for bronze-tinted foliage and warm apricot-orange blooms, it flowers over a long summer season. Twist and pull spent stems from the base to keep new flowering shoots coming.

planting.note: Plant compact Inca-series divisions after spring frost.

directions: Plant this dwarf, container-friendly selection with its crown an inch or two below the surface in free-draining soil. Its peachy-cream flowers flushed with rose and gold suit patio pots and front borders. Deadhead by pulling stems to prolong the summer-long display.

planting.note: Set dwarf Princess-series plants out after the last frost.

directions: Plant these compact, patio-sized alstroemerias with the crown just below soil level in a sunny, well-drained spot. Bred for long bloom in containers, they flower from early summer into autumn if kept watered. Pull old flower stems from the base rather than cutting to spur repeat blooming.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost; among the more cold-tolerant selections.

directions: Plant the roots several inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil, spreading them out carefully. Notable for lightly fragrant golden-yellow flowers and improved winter hardiness. Mulch the crown in autumn and pull stems at the base when harvesting through the summer bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions out after the spring frost has passed.

directions: Set the crown a couple of inches deep in rich, moisture-retentive but well-drained soil. This selection carries deep purple-magenta blooms with the classic streaked throat on long, cut-flower-quality stems. Twist stems free at the base to keep flowers coming all summer.

planting.note: Plant tuberous roots in spring; spreads readily in mild climates.

directions: Plant the roots about 6 inches deep in a spot with room to spread, as it colonizes freely. The exotic dark-red and green tubular flowers appear in mid to late summer and are excellent for cutting. Watch its vigorous spread in mild regions where it can naturalize.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost once soil is warm.

directions: Plant rhizomes horizontally about 4 inches deep with the growing points facing up in rich, moist soil. Give them heat, ample water, and feeding for lush tropical foliage and tall flower spikes. Deadhead spent blooms and lift rhizomes before frost in cold regions; flowers appear from summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring, or fall in frost-free areas.

directions: Plant corms pointed-end up about 2 inches deep in a sunny, well-drained bed or container. Prized for intensely fragrant, one-sided sprays of funnel-shaped flowers on wiry stems. Provide cool nights for best flowering and support the floppy stems as buds develop.

planting.note: Plant tubers only after soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Plant tubers knobby-side up about 2 inches deep only once soil has warmed above 70 degrees, as cold ground rots them. Grown for heart-shaped leaves splashed in red, pink, and white rather than flowers. Keep moist and shaded; lift and store tubers dry before cool weather returns.

planting.note: Pot bulbs any time indoors, or plant outdoors in frost-free zones.

directions: Plant the large bulb with its top third above the soil in a snug pot of well-drained mix. Water sparingly until the flower stalk emerges, then keep evenly moist as the huge trumpet flowers open. After bloom, feed the foliage to rebuild the bulb, then give it a dry rest to trigger reblooming.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for autumn flowers.

directions: Plant corms about 3 to 4 inches deep in late summer, and they may bloom within weeks of planting. Leafless goblet-shaped pink flowers rise in autumn, with the broad leaves following in spring. All parts are highly poisonous, so site them away from vegetable beds and handle with care.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall for late-spring bloom.

directions: Plant corms about 3 to 4 inches deep in fall in well-drained soil that dries out in summer. Loose umbels of violet-blue star flowers top slender stems in late spring and early summer. This drought-tolerant western native thrives with a dry dormant period and makes a good cut flower.

planting.note: Plant the fleshy crowns shallowly in fall on a bed of sand.

directions: Set the star-shaped fleshy root crown just below the surface on a handful of coarse sand to ensure sharp drainage. Tall spires packed with tiny yellow flowers rise in early summer, opening from the bottom up. Protect the brittle roots from disturbance and mark their spot, as they emerge late.

planting.note: Plant soaked corms in autumn in mild climates or early spring where winters are hard.

directions: Soak the knobby corms in water for a few hours, then set them about two inches deep in fast-draining soil with the pointed side down. Provide steady moisture through the growing season and shelter from harsh midday heat. Expect the bright poppy-like blooms roughly three to four months after planting, and cut freely to encourage more buds.

planting.note: Set corms out in fall in zone 8 and warmer, or early spring in colder gardens.

directions: Rehydrate the corms briefly before planting them two inches deep in rich, well-drained ground. This single-flowered selection opens pure white petals around a dark central boss and prefers cool growing weather. Harvest stems just as the buds begin to color for the longest vase life.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn where winters are mild, otherwise wait until early spring.

directions: Soak corms a few hours and bury them two inches deep in gritty, moisture-retentive soil. This variety carries luminous violet-blue single flowers on wiry stems that suit both borders and cutting rows. Keep plants evenly watered and pick blooms often through spring.

planting.note: Corms go out in fall in warm zones or early spring elsewhere.

directions: Presoak the corms, then plant them two inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained bed. The scarlet-red single flowers show a striking white ring and black eye that draws attention in any planting. Water steadily and deadhead or cut stems to prolong the show.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in mild regions, spring where the ground freezes.

directions: After a short soak, set corms two inches below the surface in loose, fertile soil. This selection produces vivid rosy-magenta single blooms that pair well with white companions. Give it cool conditions and consistent water, cutting flowers as buds crack open.

planting.note: Set corms in fall in warm zones or in early spring in colder gardens.

directions: Soak the corms and plant them about two inches deep in rich, well-drained soil. This St. Brigid type bears semi-double to double violet-blue flowers packed with ruffled petals. Keep the roots cool and moist, and cut stems freely once color appears.

planting.note: Plant in autumn where winters are gentle, spring where they are not.

directions: Rehydrate corms briefly, then plant two inches deep in fertile, free-draining ground. Bright scarlet semi-double flowers with a white-ringed dark eye rise on sturdy stems. Provide even moisture during growth and harvest blooms just as they unfurl.

planting.note: Corms are planted in fall in mild zones or early spring in colder areas.

directions: Soak corms for several hours and set them two inches deep in loose, humus-rich soil. This variety opens crowded double white flowers that glow against dark foliage. Water regularly through the cool season and cut stems to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn under cover or in early spring outdoors.

directions: Soak the corms and plant them two inches deep in rich, cool soil, ideally in a tunnel or sheltered bed for cut flowers. This florist strain yields large, long-stemmed deep blue blooms prized for arrangements. Keep temperatures cool and harvest as the flowers just begin to open.

planting.note: Corms are set out in autumn in mild climates or early spring elsewhere.

directions: Rehydrate corms and plant them two inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. This cut-flower selection shows rich purple petals with a pale, contrasting center. Keep plants cool and evenly watered, and pick long stems as buds start to show color.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in warm regions or early spring where winters are cold.

directions: Soak corms briefly, then plant two inches deep in loose, well-drained soil. This warm-climate strain flowers early and heavily with crisp white single blooms. Maintain steady moisture and cut stems often to keep new flowers coming.

planting.note: Set tubers in fall well before hard frost so roots can establish.

directions: Soak tubers overnight, then plant them a couple inches deep in fertile, well-drained woodland soil. This mix opens in a range of soft to deep blue tones with sunny yellow centers. Allow the foliage to die back naturally after the early-spring bloom to feed next year's display.

planting.note: Plant tubers in autumn several weeks ahead of the first hard freeze.

directions: Soak the tubers overnight and set them two to three inches deep in loose, leaf-mold soil. Large pure white flowers with reverse-tinted pink backs cover the low mounds in spring. Leave the leaves to yellow and fade so the tubers can bulk up for the following year.

planting.note: Tubers go in during fall before the ground freezes hard.

directions: Rehydrate the tubers overnight and plant them shallowly in rich, well-drained ground. Soft pink daisy flowers with white-ringed centers open across the low foliage in early spring. Let the plants naturalize undisturbed, where they will slowly spread into drifts.

planting.note: Plant tubers in autumn several weeks before hard frost sets in.

directions: Soak the tubers overnight and plant them two inches deep in humus-rich soil at a border front or under shrubs. Vivid magenta-red petals surround a bold white eye for a striking spring contrast. Allow foliage to ripen fully before it dies back to strengthen the tubers.

planting.note: Set tubers out in fall so they root before winter.

directions: Soak tubers overnight and plant them shallowly in fertile, free-draining woodland soil. Deep rose-pink flowers brighten the low spring carpet where they naturalize over time. Leave the dying leaves in place until they yellow to feed the tubers.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in autumn in a cool, partly shaded spot.

directions: Set the slender rhizomes horizontally about two inches deep in humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil. This old selection bears soft lavender-blue flowers over ferny foliage in spring. The plants go dormant by early summer, so mark their spot to avoid disturbing them.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in fall in cool woodland conditions.

directions: Lay the thin rhizomes just below the surface in leafy, moist soil under trees or shrubs. This choice form opens double white flowers with a neat central pompon of petaloids. Let the foliage die back naturally after the spring show as the plant slips into summer dormancy.

planting.note: Rhizomes are best planted in fall in shaded, cool ground.

directions: Plant the delicate rhizomes horizontally about two inches deep in rich, damp woodland soil. Fully double white flowers appear in spring above the divided leaves. The clump spreads gently over the years, then rests underground through summer.

planting.note: Plant the knobby tubers in autumn before the ground freezes.

directions: Soak the small tubers overnight and plant them two to three inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in dappled shade. Sky-blue daisy flowers with narrow petals open in spring and naturalize over time. Allow the foliage to fade on its own before the plant rests through summer.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in mild zones or early spring in colder gardens.

directions: Soak the corms briefly and plant them about two inches deep in sharply drained, sunny soil. Brilliant scarlet single flowers with dark centers appear in spring on slender stems. Keep the soil moist during growth and cut blooms to enjoy indoors.

planting.note: Set corms out in autumn where winters are mild, spring where they are cold.

directions: Soak the corms for a few hours and plant them two inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. Broad single flowers in shades of scarlet, pink, and purple, often with a pale ring, open in spring. Provide cool growing conditions and steady moisture for the best blooms.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in warm regions or early spring elsewhere.

directions: Soak corms and plant them two inches deep in free-draining, sunny soil. Star-shaped flowers with many narrow petals appear in shades of rosy purple to lilac in spring. Keep the roots cool and evenly watered during the growing season.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in mild climates or early spring where the ground freezes.

directions: Soak the corms for several hours, then plant them two inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. This classic single-flowered group blooms in a mix of red, blue, white, and pink poppy-like flowers. Grow in cool weather with steady water and cut stems freely for arrangements.

planting.note: Corms are planted in autumn in warm zones or early spring in colder ones.

directions: Rehydrate the corms and set them two inches deep in rich, sharply drained soil. This group produces semi-double and double flowers in a lively blend of colors on strong stems. Keep the plants cool and moist, cutting blooms as they begin to open for the vase.

planting.note: Plant tubers in fall a few weeks before hard frost.

directions: Soak the small tubers overnight and set them two inches deep in leafy, well-drained soil. Clear pink daisy flowers open across the low foliage in early spring. Leave the plants undisturbed to naturalize into soft spring drifts over the years.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in autumn in cool, shaded ground.

directions: Lay the slim rhizomes horizontally about two inches deep in moist, humus-rich woodland soil. This robust form bears large lavender-blue flowers with darker reverses in spring. Let the foliage die down naturally as the plant enters its summer rest.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in mild regions or early spring in colder gardens.

directions: Soak corms briefly and set them two inches deep in loose, well-drained soil. This early, free-flowering strain blooms in a soft blend of pastel pink, lavender, and cream tones. Keep plants cool and evenly watered, cutting stems as the buds show color.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in mild-winter regions or in early spring further north.

directions: Rehydrate the corms in cool water for several hours before planting them two inches deep, claws facing down. Grow them on in bright light with steady but not soggy moisture. Expect fully double, camellia-form flowers in a wide color range from mid to late spring; harvest stems when the buds are colored but not yet fully open for the longest vase life.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in cool conditions well ahead of setting plants out.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in cool trays and keep them out of high heat, which suppresses germination. Grow the young plants in bright, airy conditions and transplant after frost danger eases. This compact seed strain forms tidy mounds of fully double flowers in spring, making it a good choice for pots and border edges.

planting.note: An early series suited to fall planting in mild climates for late-winter bloom.

directions: Presoak corms until they swell, then set them two inches deep with the fingers pointing down. This early-flowering series responds well to cool growing temperatures and consistent moisture. Cut the fully double blooms as color shows for reliable, uniform stems through spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in frost-free areas or early spring where the ground freezes.

directions: Soak the clawed corms briefly and plant them two inches down, prongs facing the soil. This cut-flower series is prized for long, strong stems and large double blooms. Keep plants cool and evenly watered, and harvest when the outer petals loosen but the center stays tight.

planting.note: A cloned cut-flower series planted in fall in mild zones for spring stems.

directions: Rehydrate the corms for a few hours, then plant them two inches deep in well-drained soil. This Italian bred series produces uniform, densely petaled flowers on tall stems built for cutting. Grow cool and bright, and pick blooms as the buds begin to unfurl their color.

planting.note: Premium cloned corms grown for large, long-lasting cut flowers.

directions: Soak the corms until firm and plump, then set them two inches deep with claws pointing down. This selection is known for extra-large, tightly doubled blooms in rich saturated tones. Provide cool temperatures and even moisture, and cut stems just as the flowers start to open for maximum vase life.

planting.note: A novelty series with shaggy, multi-toned pompon flowers.

directions: Presoak the clawed corms and plant them two inches deep in loose, fast-draining soil. This series carries frilly, densely petaled pompon blooms often streaked or edged in a second color. Keep the plants cool and steadily moist, and harvest as the ruffled buds show full color.

planting.note: A Japanese-bred series noted for very full, rounded flower heads.

directions: Soak the corms for several hours, then plant them two inches deep with the prongs facing down. This series is valued for exceptionally full, ball-shaped blooms packed with petals. Grow cool and bright with consistent moisture, and cut stems as the tight buds begin to loosen.

planting.note: Italian-bred single-form hybrids that bloom over a long window.

directions: Rehydrate the corms and plant them two inches deep, spacing them a bit wider than double types. Unlike the full doubles, these carry semi-double to single, shimmering flowers held on branching, wiry stems. They produce many buds per plant over a long season, so cut or deadhead regularly to keep new blooms coming.

planting.note: A named Butterfly selection in warm rosy-pink tones.

directions: Soak the corms until plump, then plant them two inches deep with the claws pointing down. This Butterfly selection opens rosy-pink single flowers with glossy petals over a long bloom period. Keep plants cool and evenly watered, and cut often to encourage the branching stems to keep budding.

planting.note: A named Butterfly selection in deep wine-red to near-black tones.

directions: Presoak the corms and set them two inches deep in loose, well-drained soil. This selection carries dark wine-red single blooms with a shimmering sheen on branching stems. Grow cool and bright, and pick or deadhead frequently to prolong the flowering run.

planting.note: A named Butterfly selection in bright golden-yellow.

directions: Rehydrate the corms for a few hours, then plant them two inches deep with the prongs facing down. This selection produces glowing golden-yellow single flowers over branching, wiry stems. Keep the soil cool and evenly moist, and harvest stems as the buds show color to keep the plant flowering.

planting.note: A named Butterfly selection in deep pink and rose shades.

directions: Soak the clawed corms until swollen, then plant them two inches deep in fast-draining soil. This selection opens richly saturated pink single blooms with a satiny finish. Grow cool and bright, and cut regularly to keep the branching stems generating fresh buds.

planting.note: A named Butterfly selection in soft peach and apricot tones.

directions: Presoak the corms and plant them two inches deep with the claws pointing downward. This selection blooms in soft peach-apricot single flowers that shift subtly as they mature. Keep plants cool with steady moisture, and deadhead or cut often to extend the long flowering season.

planting.note: A named Butterfly selection in purple to lavender tones.

directions: Soak the corms until firm, then set them two inches deep in loose, well-drained soil. This selection carries luminous purple single flowers on airy, branching stems. Grow cool and bright, and cut stems as the buds begin to open to encourage repeat blooming.

planting.note: A named Butterfly selection in dark purple-burgundy shades.

directions: Rehydrate the corms and plant them two inches deep with the prongs facing the soil. This selection opens deep purple-burgundy single flowers with a subtle shimmer. Keep the plants cool and evenly watered, and harvest frequently to keep the branching stems producing.

planting.note: A named Butterfly selection in coral to warm orange tones.

directions: Soak the corms for several hours, then plant them two inches deep in well-drained soil. This selection produces warm coral-orange single flowers that glow against the fine foliage. Grow cool and bright, and cut or deadhead often to sustain the long bloom period.

planting.note: A named Butterfly selection in clean white.

directions: Presoak the corms until plump and plant them two inches deep with the claws pointing down. This selection opens crisp white single flowers with a soft sheen on branching stems. Keep the soil cool and consistently moist, and cut regularly to promote a steady succession of buds.

planting.note: A named Butterfly selection in soft pastel pink.

directions: Soak the corms until they swell, then plant them two inches deep with the prongs facing down. This selection carries delicate pastel-pink single flowers on wiry, branching stems. Grow cool and bright with even moisture, and harvest as color shows to keep the plant flowering longer.

planting.note: A named Butterfly selection in pale blush and cream tones.

directions: Rehydrate the corms and plant them two inches deep in loose, fast-draining soil. This selection opens soft blush-cream single flowers with a gentle glow. Keep plants cool and evenly watered, and cut or deadhead often to extend the flowering run across spring.

planting.note: A named Butterfly selection in bright rose-pink.

directions: Soak the clawed corms until plump and set them two inches deep with the claws pointing down. This selection produces vivid rose-pink single flowers over a long season on branching stems. Grow cool and bright, and pick regularly to keep fresh buds forming.

planting.note: An early cut-flower series well suited to fall planting in mild zones.

directions: Soak the corms until firm, then plant them two inches deep with the prongs facing down. This early series delivers uniform double flowers in a broad palette and tolerates cool growing conditions well. Keep the soil evenly moist, and cut the stems as color appears for dependable spring bouquets.

planting.note: A large-flowered series known for ruffled, picotee-edged blooms.

directions: Presoak the corms for a few hours and plant them two inches deep in loose soil. This series is grown for oversized, frilled flowers often finished with a contrasting picotee edge. Provide cool temperatures and steady moisture, and harvest as the ruffled buds start to open.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring after frost, or in autumn in frost-free gardens.

directions: Set corms about 2 inches deep with the pointed end up in cool, well-drained soil. Provide a stake or grid support since the fragrant, arching flower spikes tend to flop as they fill out. Blooms open in sequence along one side of each stem roughly three to four months after planting.

planting.note: In mild climates plant corms in autumn for late-winter to spring bloom.

directions: Plant corms 2 inches deep in sandy, fast-draining soil and keep barely moist until shoots appear. This wild species carries intensely scented creamy-white flowers marked with yellow throats. Ease off watering once the foliage yellows so the corms can rest through summer dormancy.

planting.note: Autumn planting suits frost-free zones; lift or grow in pots elsewhere.

directions: Tuck corms 2 inches into gritty soil in a spot that stays cool while growing. The pale yellow, sweetly scented blooms open along wiry stems in spring. Allow the plants to dry off after flowering and store dormant corms warm and dry.

planting.note: Plant in autumn where winters are frost-free for spring flowers.

directions: Plant corms about 2 inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained bed or container. This South African species bears pink to yellow flowers with contrasting throats on short, upright stems. Water while in active growth and withhold moisture during summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring; this species self-sows freely in mild gardens.

directions: Set the small corms an inch or two deep in humus-rich soil that drains well. Star-shaped red flowers with darker basal blotches appear on delicate stems through summer. It naturalizes readily from seed, so leave a few pods to ripen if you want a spreading colony.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring, or in autumn in frost-free areas.

directions: Plant corms 2 inches deep, pointed end up, in cool, well-drained soil. This selection produces large, fragrant, pure white single flowers ideal for cutting. Stake the stems early and harvest spikes when the lowest bud shows color for the longest vase life.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring after frost, or in autumn where winters stay mild.

directions: Set corms 2 inches deep in fertile, freely draining soil and keep them cool during growth. This variety opens rich red single flowers with a golden-yellow throat. Provide support as the spikes lengthen and pick stems just as the first buds crack open.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring, or autumn in frost-free gardens.

directions: Plant corms about 2 inches deep in cool, well-drained ground and water steadily during growth. The double, many-petaled lavender-blue flowers are heavily scented and hold well when cut. Stake the flopping stems and let foliage die back naturally after bloom.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn several weeks before the ground freezes.

directions: Plant bulbs about 6 to 8 inches deep with the pointed end up in well-drained soil. This classic Darwin hybrid opens large, glossy scarlet cups on sturdy stems in mid spring. A period of winter cold is required, so leave the bulbs in place or refrigerate them where winters are warm.

planting.note: Plant in autumn so roots establish before winter.

directions: Set bulbs roughly three times their height deep in fertile, well-drained soil. This all-yellow trumpet daffodil naturalizes well and returns reliably each spring. Let the foliage yellow and die down on its own to recharge the bulb for next year.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost and stagger plantings for a long cut-flower run.

directions: Plant corms 4 to 6 inches deep in fertile soil once the ground has warmed. Tall spikes of ruffled flowers open from the bottom up in summer and make superb cut flowers. In cold zones lift and dry the corms after the first frost and store them frost-free over winter.

planting.note: Plant tubers after the last frost once the soil has warmed.

directions: Lay tubers on their side about 4 to 6 inches deep with the eye facing up and do not water heavily until sprouts emerge. Pinch the growing tips to encourage bushier plants and more blooms from midsummer to frost. In cold climates dig the tubers after frost blackens the tops and store them in a cool, dry place.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn or early spring; they resent drying out.

directions: Plant the fleshy bulbs about 6 inches deep in rich soil that drains well, keeping the roots cool and the tops sunny. This famous cultivar bears upward-facing crimson-and-white flowers with a powerful fragrance in mid to late summer. Leave at least two-thirds of the stem when cutting so the bulb can rebuild.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs in autumn for very early flowers.

directions: Set the little bulbs about 3 to 4 inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil. Jewel-toned blue and purple flowers open close to the ground in late winter, before most other bulbs. Give the plants a dry summer rest so they return dependably.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Plant corms about 3 to 5 inches deep in groups in fertile, well-drained soil. Arching sprays of fiery red flowers open along the stems in mid to late summer and draw hummingbirds. Clumps bulk up quickly, so lift and divide them every few years to keep them flowering freely.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild climates, or spring where winters are cold.

directions: Plant corms about 2 to 3 inches deep in sandy, sharply drained soil in full sun. Wiry stems carry starry flowers, often with a dark eye, that open wide in bright sunshine during late spring. Let the plants dry off after bloom and keep the corms warm and dry through their summer rest.

planting.note: Plant tubers only after soil is thoroughly warm; they sulk in cold ground.

directions: Plant tubers about 2 inches deep, knobby side up, only once the soil is warm and settled. Grown for their heart-shaped leaves splashed in red, pink, and white rather than for flowers, they thrive in warm, humid shade with steady moisture. Lift and dry the tubers before frost if you want to keep them for another year.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring with the neck at or just above soil level.

directions: Plant bulbs shallowly with their necks poking above the soil surface at the foot of a warm wall. Umbels of frilled pink flowers appear on bare stems in autumn, often after the leaves. Leave the bulbs undisturbed and let them bake in summer, as crowded clumps bloom best.

planting.note: Pot bulbs any time indoors; blooms follow six to ten weeks later.

directions: Pot the large bulb with the top third sitting above the soil and water sparingly until growth begins. Tall hollow stems carry several huge trumpet flowers, often in red, pink, or white, within a couple of months. After bloom keep the leaves growing in bright light, then give the bulb a dry dormant rest to trigger the next round of flowers.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in soil that stays moist in spring.

directions: Plant bulbs about 4 inches deep in fertile soil that holds moisture through spring but is not waterlogged in summer. Tall spires of starry blue or white flowers open in late spring and suit meadow and prairie plantings. Unlike many bulbs it tolerates heavier, damp ground and naturalizes over time.

planting.note: Plant deeply in fall in a spot with excellent drainage.

directions: Bed the heavy bulbs 6 to 8 inches deep on a handful of coarse sand and angle them so the crown sheds rain. Feed in early spring and stake exposed plantings against wind. This selection carries a bold whorl of burnt orange-red bells in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in fertile, free-draining ground.

directions: Sink the bulbs 6 to 8 inches deep, tipped slightly to keep the crown dry, and mulch lightly the first winter. Avoid disturbing established clumps, which resent transplanting. Clear golden-yellow bells ring the top of the stem in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall while soil is still workable.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 6 inches deep on their side in rich, well-drained soil. This is a more compact selection that stands better without staking. Its deep orange bells nod in a tight crown in mid-spring.

planting.note: Autumn-plant in deep, humus-rich soil.

directions: Set bulbs 6 to 8 inches deep, angled to protect the crown, and keep them evenly moist through spring growth. Let leaves ripen fully before tidying. Soft tangerine-orange bells with delicate veining appear in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sheltered, well-drained bed.

directions: Bury the bulbs 6 to 8 inches deep on their side and grow them in fertile soil that never stays soggy. This unusual form stacks two tiers of bells separated by a whorl of leaves. The doubled orange crowns unfurl in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant deep in fall for spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 to 8 inches deep, tilted to drain the crown, in ground improved with grit and compost. Allow the strap leaves to yellow before removing them. Richly colored bronze-orange bells with darker veins ring the stem in mid-spring.

planting.note: Autumn plant in a bright, sheltered position.

directions: Set bulbs 6 to 8 inches deep in gritty, fertile soil and keep the crown from collecting water. Prized for foliage edged in creamy white beneath orange bells. Blooms open in mid-spring above the striking variegated leaves.

planting.note: Plant in fall in rich, draining soil.

directions: Plant the bulbs 6 to 8 inches deep, angled to shed rain, and leave clumps undisturbed once settled. Feed lightly as shoots emerge. Soft apricot-yellow bells finely netted with orange veining appear in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn before hard frost.

directions: Bed bulbs about 6 to 7 inches deep on their side in free-draining soil. This vigorous, sturdy-stemmed form needs little staking. Bright orange bells crown the stem in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall for an early spring display.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 to 8 inches deep, tilted so the crown drains, in fertile, gritty ground. As the name suggests, it flowers a little ahead of most crown imperials. Warm orange bells open in early to mid-spring.

planting.note: Autumn-plant in a warm, well-drained border.

directions: Set bulbs 6 to 8 inches deep on their side in soil amended with grit. Grown for its striped foliage as much as its flowers. Orange bells rise above the patterned leaves in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in deep, fertile soil.

directions: Plant the large bulbs 7 to 8 inches deep, angled to keep the crown dry, and give them a sheltered site. Let foliage die down on its own after flowering. Tall stems bear a crown of deep orange-red bells in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in moisture-retentive ground.

directions: Set the little bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in cool, humus-rich soil and let them naturalize undisturbed. They pair well with the checkered species in grass. Ivory-white bells with a faint green checker nod in early to mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in cool, damp soil.

directions: Plant the small bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in moist, well-drained soil and leave them to settle. This selection carries clean, pure white bells without checkering. Flowers nod on slender stems in early to mid-spring.

planting.note: Autumn-plant in moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant the bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in cool ground that does not dry out in spring. Best naturalized where the deep color can be seen up close. Dark, near-blackish purple bells open in early to mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in cool, damp soil.

directions: Set the bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil and let them naturalize. This selection bears larger-than-typical bells in a deep reddish-purple. Flowers appear in early to mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for an early spring show.

directions: Plant the small bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in cool, moist soil and leave them undisturbed to spread. This selection tends to flower a touch earlier. Reddish-violet bells nod on wiry stems in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a hot, sharply drained site.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 6 inches deep, angled slightly, in lean soil with excellent drainage and full sun, ideally against a warm wall. Keep them dry during summer dormancy. Tall spires hung with dozens of small plum-purple bells rise in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a warm, well-drained spot.

directions: Set the large bulbs about 6 inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil and give them all the sun and summer warmth you can. This robust selection is taller and more richly colored than the type. Dense spires of deep aubergine bells open in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs about 6 inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained bed and keep them dry through summer. This pale form is a striking contrast to the usual dark type. Spires of soft greenish-ivory bells appear in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the small bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in a raised bed or trough with sharp drainage and full sun. Keep them on the dry side when dormant in summer. Compact stems carry maroon bells tipped in bright chrome-yellow in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in free-draining soil in sun or light shade; this species increases freely by offsets. Leave clumps to build up over several seasons. Slender stems hold narrow purple-brown bells tipped with mustard-yellow in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in cool, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant bulbs about 4 to 5 inches deep in cool, moisture-retentive soil in light shade; this species tolerates cold well and dislikes drought. Leave it undisturbed to form colonies. Broad, pale creamy-yellow bells lightly checkered inside open in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant the bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil in full sun and keep them dry in summer. This obliging species settles in easily and multiplies. Green bells stained mahogany-brown, with slightly reflexed tips, nod in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in leafy, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil in dappled shade at a woodland edge. Let it seed about gently to form a colony. Soft green bells flushed with brownish-purple at the rim hang among leafy bracts in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained spot; this is one of the easier species for the open garden. Leave it to settle and multiply slowly. Solitary dark maroon-purple bells, chequered and yellow-green within, nod in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in cool, moist, acidic soil.

directions: Plant the scaly bulbs about 4 inches deep in cool, humus-rich soil that stays evenly moist, in part shade. It thrives where summers are not baking hot. Whorled leafy stems carry deep chocolate-black bells in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in gritty soil that dries out in summer.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 4 inches deep in fast-draining soil in light shade, mimicking the dry summer of its western woodland home. Keep it dry once foliage fades. Nodding bells mottled green and brownish-purple open along the stem in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in sharply drained soil kept dry in summer.

directions: Plant the bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in lean, gritty soil in sun to light shade and enforce a dry summer rest. Best in a raised bed or pot in wetter climates. One to a few brownish, greenish-veined bells nod in early to mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in gritty soil with a dry summer.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 4 inches deep in sharply drained soil in light shade and keep them dry during summer dormancy. This is a choice but somewhat particular species. Slender stems carry recurved scarlet-orange bells checkered with yellow in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a warm, very well-drained site.

directions: Plant the large bulbs about 6 inches deep, tilted to drain the crown, in gritty soil and full sun. It resembles a smaller, softer-colored crown imperial and flowers early. A whorl of pale primrose-yellow bells beneath a leafy tuft opens in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 4 to 5 inches deep in rich, free-draining soil in sun or light shade. The upper leaves end in curling tendrils that let the plant lean on neighbors. Pale creamy-green bells faintly netted within hang in the leaf axils in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in gritty, sun-warmed soil.

directions: Plant the bulbs 3 to 4 inches deep in sharply drained soil in full sun and keep them dry through summer. It suits a bulb frame or gravelly bed. Narrow bells striped green and deep purple-brown nod on slim stems in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in gritty alpine soil.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 3 inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun, as in a trough or scree bed. This alpine dislikes winter wet at the crown. A single broad, purple-pink bell faintly checkered and grey-bloomed outside opens in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a very sharply drained bed.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about 3 inches deep in very gritty, free-draining soil in full sun, ideally in a trough or bulb frame. Keep it dry once dormant in summer. Dwarf stems hold a golden-yellow bell lightly chequered with orange-brown in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 3 inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil in full sun and keep them dry during their summer rest. This early Central Asian species suits a bulb frame or alpine house. Small pale pink bells with a darker central band open in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in gritty, free-draining soil.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 4 inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in sun or light shade and give it a dry summer dormancy. This distinctive species carries its bells in the leaf axils up the stem. Narrow greenish bells flushed dusky purple appear in early to mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 4 inches deep in free-draining soil in sun or light shade; the upper leaves are whorled and end in curling tendrils. Let it establish undisturbed. Creamy-white bells lightly checkered in green nod along the stem in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in sharply drained, sun-warmed soil.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about 3 inches deep in very gritty soil in full sun and keep them dry in summer. This low Greek species is well suited to a raised bed or pot. Broad brown-green bells chequered inside nod just above the ground in early to mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 3 inches deep in sharply drained soil in full sun and enforce a dry summer rest. It grows well in a trough or bulb frame. Green bells marked with a broad greenish central stripe and chequering open in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in gritty, free-draining soil.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 3 inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun and keep them dry through summer dormancy. This modest Turkish species is best in a pot or raised bed. Small greenish-yellow bells nod on short stems in early to mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in very sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 3 inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil in full sun and give a dry summer rest. Its fleshy leaves suit a bulb frame or trough. Green bells chequered with dull purple-brown and yellow nod on short stems in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in early autumn in a spot that stays reasonably dry through summer dormancy.

directions: Set the plump bulbs three to four inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil that catches winter sun. This species carries broad grey-green leaves and larger flowers than the common snowdrop, with two green marks often merging on the inner segments. Expect the first blooms in mid to late winter, sometimes ahead of Galanthus nivalis.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in soil that holds some summer moisture; it tolerates damper ground than most snowdrops.

directions: Plant bulbs around three inches deep in leafy, humus-rich soil in light shade. The glossy bright-green leaves are the standout feature and set off the simple white flowers well. Blooms appear in late winter to early spring and the clumps increase steadily once settled.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in early autumn in fertile, free-draining soil under a light tree canopy.

directions: Bury the bulbs three to four inches deep in ground enriched with leaf mould. Named for the folded-back margins of its broad grey leaves, this vigorous species naturalizes readily and stands up well to cold. The green-tipped white flowers open from late winter into early spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer to early autumn in a warm, sharply drained spot.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in gritty soil at the base of a sunny wall where summers stay dry. Unusually, the flowers appear in autumn, often before the leaves fully develop. Give it the warmest, best-drained position you have and avoid summer irrigation.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in cool, moisture-retentive soil in dappled shade.

directions: Set bulbs three inches deep in leaf-rich soil that never bakes dry. The broad, deep-green leaves and boldly green-marked inner segments distinguish this Aegean island species. Flowers open in late winter to early spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a sunny, well-drained position that dries out in summer.

directions: Plant the bulbs about three inches deep in gritty soil in a warm spot. Narrow, slightly twisted grey-green leaves and a mark on both inner and sometimes outer segments make it easy to recognize. It flowers in late winter and appreciates a dry summer rest.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in early autumn in humus-rich, well-drained soil in light shade.

directions: Bury bulbs around three inches deep in cool, leafy soil beneath deciduous cover. This compact Black Sea species has narrow dark-green leaves and a single green mark at the tip of each inner segment. Flowers open in mid to late winter.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in well-drained soil in a lightly shaded, sheltered spot.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in gritty, humus-enriched soil. This Caucasian species has bright glossy-green leaves and a neat green mark on each inner segment. It flowers reliably in late winter once established.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a warm, sharply drained position; it dislikes cold wet soil.

directions: Set bulbs three inches deep in free-draining, gritty soil in a sheltered warm spot, ideally under glass or against a wall in cold gardens. The broad glossy leaves and large flowers with two green marks on each inner segment are handsome but need protection from prolonged wet. Flowers open in late winter.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer in a hot, dry, sharply drained spot such as a raised bed or bulb frame.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in very gritty soil where they can bake dry in summer. This autumn-flowering species from the eastern Mediterranean opens its slender white flowers in October and November, usually before or with the leaves. It needs the warmest, driest position you can offer and resents winter wet.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in humus-rich, well-drained soil in light shade.

directions: Bury bulbs roughly three inches deep in cool, leafy soil beneath deciduous shrubs. This Caucasian species carries dull grey-green leaves and small green-tipped flowers. It blooms in late winter and settles into steady clumps over a few seasons.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn or divide clumps in the green after flowering.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil in dappled shade. This old double form packs a ruffled rosette of green-tipped inner segments and bulks up quickly into generous drifts. It flowers in late winter and is one of the easiest doubles to grow.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in light shade.

directions: Set bulbs three inches deep in leafy soil beneath deciduous shrubs. This robust selection is prized for the green blotch at the tip of each outer segment, giving the flower a distinctive two-tone look. It grows a touch taller than the type and blooms in late winter.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in humus-rich soil in a partly shaded border.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in cool, leaf-rich soil. This curiosity is known for its two upright, ear-like green spathe segments that stand above the flower like rabbit ears, plus green-tipped outer segments. It flowers in late winter and clumps up steadily.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn, or lift and divide clumps in the green just after flowering.

directions: Plant bulbs three to four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in light shade. This celebrated cultivar bears large, rounded, honey-scented flowers on strong stems and is one of the most reliable garden snowdrops. Enjoy the fragrant blooms in late winter and lift congested clumps every few years to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in fertile soil in a partly shaded, sheltered spot.

directions: Set bulbs three to four inches deep in humus-rich, free-draining soil. This tall, vigorous old cultivar produces elegant elongated flowers early in the season and multiplies fast into impressive clumps. Watch for the occasional irregular flower with extra segments; blooms open in early to mid winter.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in well-drained, humus-rich soil in light shade.

directions: Plant bulbs three to four inches deep in leafy, free-draining soil. This distinctive cultivar dangles its flowers on unusually long, slender pedicels so they swing and dance in the slightest breeze. It is robust and free-increasing, flowering in late winter.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in fertile soil in a sheltered, partly shaded position.

directions: Plant bulbs three to four inches deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil. This vigorous elwesii-type cultivar is valued for its very early, large flowers, often opening around the turn of the year. Give it a sheltered spot and enjoy some of the season's first blooms.
